channel.title: Home on FsFlow
channel.description: Recent content in Home on FsFlow

item.title: FsToolkit.ErrorHandling
item.description:
If you are coming from FsToolkit.ErrorHandling, you will find that FsFlow is orthogonal rather than a direct replacement. While both libraries help with result-based programming, FsFlow focuses on a unified execution model that carries environments and runtime policies.

The Model Difference

FsToolkit.ErrorHandling provides a broad toolbox of helpers for working with Result, AsyncResult, and TaskResult as separate, wrapped types.

FsFlow provides a single, scalable progression:

Check -> Result -> Validation -> Flow

item.title: Getting Started
item.description:
FsFlow is a toolkit for building robust, Result-based programs in F#. It allows you to scale from simple validation logic to complex, effectful application boundaries using a single, unified mental model.

1. The Continuum of Logic

FsFlow is designed around a continuum. You should always use the simplest tool that satisfies your current requirement:

Pure Checks -> Result & Validation -> Flow
  • Pure Checks: Reusable predicates for basic validation.
  • Result & Validation: Domain logic that handles success or failure (either fail-fast or error-accumulating).
  • Flow: The application boundary where you need dependencies, async/task interop, logging, or cancellation.

2. Start with Checks and Results

Most logic starts pure. Use Check for reusable predicates and Result for domain logic.

item.title: Pure Checks
item.description:
Check<'value> is the foundation of FsFlow validation. It is a reusable predicate layer that returns Result<'value, unit>.

  • If the predicate is true, it returns Ok value.
  • If the predicate is false, it returns Error ().

By returning unit on failure, Check stays agnostic about your application’s error types. You decide what the error means at the boundary where you use it.

The Two Shapes of Check

  1. Value-Preserving: Check<'value> - returns the input value on success. 
    let check = Check.notBlank // string -> Result<string, unit>
  2. Gate: Check<unit> - returns unit on success (useful for yes/no questions). 
    let check = Check.okIf (age > 18) // unit -> Result<unit, unit>

Attaching Errors with Check.orError

Use Check.orError to turn a unit failure into a meaningful application error.

item.title: Ref (Atomic References)
item.description:
Ref<'T> is a handle to a mutable reference that can be updated atomically. It is the simplest way to manage shared state between multiple concurrent parts of your application.

Note: Ref is currently available on .NET only.

Why Use Ref?

In a functional program, state is usually passed as parameters or through the environment. However, some scenarios—like a shared counter, a cache, or a status flag—require multiple concurrent workflows to observe and update the same value.

item.title: Runtime vs Environment
item.description:
FsFlow models dependencies by drawing a strict line between business dependencies and operational services. This is one of the defining architectural ideas of the library, inspired by the runtime model of ZIO.

In FsFlow:

  • Business dependencies (repositories, gateways, domain services) live in the Explicit Environment.
  • Operational services (clock, logging, random numbers, scheduling) live in the Ambient Runtime.

Understanding why this distinction exists is key to writing clean, maintainable workflows that scale without suffering from type-signature bloat.

item.title: Tutorial: AppRecord
item.description:
The simplest way to manage dependencies in FsFlow is using a concrete record as your environment (env). This provides direct field access and minimal boilerplate.

In this tutorial, we will build a small order placement workflow that uses a record for its dependencies.

1. Define Your Dependencies

Start by defining the interfaces for your services and a record that bundles them together.

open System
open System.Threading.Tasks
open FsFlow

type Order = { Id: Guid; Total: decimal }

type IOrderRepository =
    abstract Save: Order -> Task<unit>

type IEmailSender =
    abstract SendConfirmation: Order -> Task<unit>

type AppEnv =
    { Orders: IOrderRepository
      Email: IEmailSender }

2. Write the Workflow

Use the flow {} builder to define your business logic. You can use Flow.read to project specific services from the environment.

item.title: Execution and Outcomes
item.description:
Once you have defined a Flow, you must execute it to perform its effects and obtain a result. This page covers the mechanics of running flows, handling the different types of outcomes, and combining multiple flows.

1. Running a Flow

The primary entry point for execution is Flow.run. It requires an environment (even if just ()) and returns a platform-specific deferred handle called an Effect.

let myFlow = Flow.succeed "Hello"

// On .NET: returns ValueTask<Exit<string, 'error>>
let! exit = Flow.run () myFlow

// On Fable: returns Async<Exit<string, 'error>>
let! exit = Flow.run () myFlow

Unwrapping to Result

While Exit is the native outcome of a flow, you may want to bridge back to a standard F# Result. The pattern differs by platform because of the underlying threading model.

item.title: Execution Semantics
item.description:
This page shows the exact execution rules for Flow, including how a cold program becomes an Effect and resolves to an Exit.

FsFlow uses a unified Flow<'env, 'error, 'value> model that handles synchronous code, F# Async, and .NET Task interop natively.

Execution Shape

Conceptually, execution is:

Flow -> Effect -> Exit

More precisely:

  • Flow is the cold program you define.
  • Effect is the deferred runnable carrier.
  • Exit is the terminal outcome after execution.

Success and Typed Failure

Every Flow execution results in an Exit<'value, 'error>:

item.title: Result CE
item.description:
Use the result {} computation expression when you have a sequence of steps where each step depends on the previous one, and you want to stop at the first failure.

This is “fail-fast” semantics.

Basic Usage

The result {} builder binds standard F# Result<'value, 'error> types.

type UserError = | MissingName | MissingEmail

let validateUser name email =
    result {
        // If name is blank, it returns Error MissingName and stops.
        let! validName = name |> Check.notBlank |> Check.orError MissingName
        
        // This line only runs if the name was valid.
        let! validEmail = email |> Check.notBlank |> Check.orError MissingEmail
        
        return { Name = validName; Email = validEmail }
    }

Guard

result {} binds Result directly. Guard bridges Option, bool, Check, and other source shapes into result {} while attaching an error value at the binding site.

item.title: Schedule (Retry & Repeat)
item.description:
The Schedule module provides a powerful DSL for describing policies that govern how and when a workflow should be executed again. You can use schedules to implement sophisticated retry strategies (like exponential backoff with jitter) or recurring tasks.

Note: Schedule is currently available on .NET only.

Basic Schedules

A schedule decides two things:

  1. Whether to continue (recur).
  2. How long to wait before the next attempt.

Fixed Number of Recursions

// Recur 5 times (6 attempts total)
let fiveTimes = Schedule.recurs 5

Fixed Spacing

// Recur indefinitely with 1 second between attempts
let everySecond = Schedule.spaced (TimeSpan.FromSeconds 1.0)

Exponential Backoff

// Delays: 100ms, 200ms, 400ms, 800ms...
let backoff = Schedule.exponential (TimeSpan.FromMilliseconds 100.0)

Adding Jitter

Jitter adds randomness to delays to prevent “thundering herd” problems in distributed systems. Schedule.jittered adds a random factor between 0.5x and 1.5x to the current delay.

item.title: Troubleshooting Types
item.description:
This page shows the compiler errors that usually mean you crossed a wrapper boundary in the wrong place.

Most FsFlow type errors are not exotic. The compiler usually sees one wrapper shape and you intended another.

Error: A Unique Overload For Method Bind Could Not Be Determined

This usually happens when the compiler cannot tell which wrapper shape a let! value should use.

Example:

let nested : Async<Async<Result<int, string>>> =
    async {
        return async { return Ok 42 }
    }

let workflow : Flow<unit, string, int> =
    flow {
        let! next = nested
        let! value = next
        return value
    }

The second let! is ambiguous.

item.title: Validus Integration
item.description:
This page shows how FsFlow can fit beside Validus validation pipelines.

Validus is a strong choice when the problem is still validation, especially when you want a richer DSL, composition, accumulation, or value-object style checks.

FsFlow can usually begin after that work is done.

Validus and FsFlow.Check fit especially well together: Validus can handle richer validation rules, while FsFlow.Check stays available for smaller pure guards that feed directly into Result, Validation, or Flow.

item.title: Fibers
item.description:
This page shows how FsFlow represents running child workflows with fibers.

In FsFlow, a Fiber is a handle to a running Flow. A flow is a cold description of work. A fiber is the hot execution that exists after that work has been started in the background.

The Mental Model

While a Flow is cold (a description of work that hasn’t started yet), a Fiber is hot (the work is currently being executed).

item.title: Defects and Exceptions
item.description:
FsFlow distinguishes between expected failures, administrative signals (interruption), and unexpected defects. This separation ensures that your domain logic remains clean while the runtime provides robust, leak-proof resource management.

Quick Start: Usage Patterns

Producing Failures

Choose the function that matches your intent:

Intent Function Outcome
Domain Error (Expected) Flow.fail "Not found" Cause.Fail "Not found"
Defect/Panic (Bug) Flow.die (exn "Database down") Cause.Die exn
Interruption Flow.interrupt or runtime cancellation Cause.Interrupt

Bridging Exceptions

Use Flow.catch to convert specific exceptions into domain errors. Exceptions not caught by the handler will remain as Cause.Die.

item.title: The Guard
item.description:
The Guard turns source shapes like Option, ValueOption, bool, Result<'value, unit>, Validation<'value, unit>, and the Async / Task / ValueTask versions of those into bindable results.

Inside a flow {}, bind the effect first, then apply Guard to the value the effect returned. That keeps the source shape explicit and keeps the domain error at the binding site.

What Guard Works On

Guard.Of works on:

  • Option<'value>
  • ValueOption<'value>
  • bool
  • Result<'value, unit>
  • Validation<'value, unit>
  • Async<Option<'value>>, Task<Option<'value>>, ValueTask<Option<'value>>
  • Async<ValueOption<'value>>, Task<ValueOption<'value>>, ValueTask<ValueOption<'value>>
  • Async<bool>, Task<bool>, ValueTask<bool>
  • Async<Result<'value, unit>>, Task<Result<'value, unit>>, ValueTask<Result<'value, unit>>

Guard.MapError works on:

item.title: Benchmarks
item.description:
This page shows the performance tradeoffs of using FsFlow compared to manual composition across the runtime shapes it supports.

The benchmark harness lives in benchmarks/FsFlow.Benchmarks/Suites.fs and the shared helpers live in benchmarks/FsFlow.Benchmarks/Common.fs. The Fable runner lives in benchmarks/FsFlow.Benchmarks.Fable/Program.fs and shares its workload definitions from benchmarks/FsFlow.Benchmarks.Fable/Shared.fs. The Fable benchmark project is self-contained by source inclusion, so it can be compiled directly with Fable against the library code in src/FsFlow. Its local tool manifest lives in benchmarks/FsFlow.Benchmarks.Fable/mise.toml, with benchmarks/FsFlow.Benchmarks.Fable/package.json for Node ESM execution and scripts/run-fable-benchmarks.sh for the target-specific runner.

item.title: STM (Software Transactional Memory)
item.description:
Software Transactional Memory (STM) is a concurrency primitive that lets you compose multiple atomic operations into a single transaction.

While Ref is perfect for updating a single variable, STM is designed for scenarios where you need to update multiple variables consistently. FsFlow ensures that the entire transaction is executed atomically, and it now supports retry / orElse style coordination for transactions that need to wait on state changes or fall back to alternate branches.

item.title: Straightforward Examples
item.description:
These examples show how to use FsFlow for common tasks without the overhead of a full application setup.

1. Simple Environment Access

Use Flow.read to project a single field from your environment record.

type Config = { ApiUrl: string }

let getUrl =
    flow {
        let! url = Flow.read _.ApiUrl
        return url
    }

let run () = task {
    let! outcome = getUrl |> Flow.run { ApiUrl = "https://api.example.com" }
    // outcome = Exit.Success "https://api.example.com"
}

2. Combining Pure Logic and Async Work

Use flow {} to mix pure Check logic, Async blocks, and other flows.

let validateId id =
    id |> okIf (id > 0) |> orError "Invalid ID"

let fetchUser id =
    async { return { Id = id; Name = "Ada" } }

let workflow id =
    flow {
        let! validId = validateId id
        let! user = fetchUser validId // Binds Async<'T> directly
        return user.Name
    }

let runWorkflow () = task {
    let! exit = workflow 42 |> Flow.run ()
    // exit = Exit.Success "Ada"
}

3. Retrying a Flow

Use the Schedule module to add operational policies like retries.

item.title: Task and Async Interop
item.description:
FsFlow provides a single, unified computation expression—flow {}—that handles synchronous code, F# Async, and .NET Task interop natively. You don’t have to choose between different builders; the same flow can orchestrate all these effect types.

Direct Binds

Inside a flow {} block, you can use let! to bind many common F# and .NET types directly. FsFlow handles the conversion to its internal execution model automatically.

Type Outcome
Flow<'env, 'error, 'value> Continues with the flow’s value.
Result<'value, 'error> Continues on Ok, short-circuits on Error.
Async<'value> Awaits the async and continues with the value.
Async<Result<'value, 'error>> Awaits the async and handles the Result outcome.
Task<'value> Awaits the task and continues with the value.
Task<Result<'value, 'error>> Awaits the task and handles the Result outcome.
ValueTask<'value> Awaits the value task and continues with the value.
ValueTask<Result<'value, 'error>> Awaits and handles the Result outcome.

Example: Mixed Orchestration

let fetchUser (id: int) : Task<User> = ...
let validate (user: User) : Result<User, string> = ...
let saveUser (user: User) : Async<unit> = ...

let processUser id =
    flow {
        // Bind a .NET Task
        let! user = fetchUser id
        
        // Bind a Result
        let! validUser = validate user
        
        // Bind an F# Async
        do! saveUser validUser
        
        return "Done"
    }

Option and ValueOption

Option<'value> and ValueOption<'value> can also be bound directly, but only if the flow’s error type is unit.

item.title: Tutorial: Capabilities
item.description:
Nominal Capability Contracts use F# interfaces to name app dependencies. This lets the compiler check that your environment implements the required capabilities and makes it easy to reuse helpers across different workflows.

In this tutorial, we will refactor our workflow to use Honest Capabilities (IHas<'T>) instead of concrete records.

1. Define Capability Interfaces

First, define your service interfaces and their corresponding IHas<'T> contracts.

open System
open System.Threading.Tasks
open FsFlow

type IOrderRepository =
    abstract member Save : Order -> unit

type IEmailSender =
    abstract member SendConfirmation : Order -> unit

// Define the "Has" contracts
type IHasOrders = inherit IHas<IOrderRepository>
type IHasEmail = inherit IHas<IEmailSender>

// You can also group them into a larger contract
type IAppCaps =
    inherit IHasOrders
    inherit IHasEmail

2. Write Helpers using Flow.service

Helper functions can now request exactly which app capabilities they need. The compiler will infer the environment requirement automatically.

item.title: Validate CE
item.description:
Use the validate {} computation expression when you have multiple independent checks and you want to collect every failure into a single report.

While the standard result {} or flow {} blocks “fail fast” (stopping at the first error), validate {} continues checking even after a failure occurs. This is often called “accumulating” semantics.

Accumulating with and!

The key to accumulation is the and! keyword. Steps joined by and! are evaluated independently, and their errors are merged into a Diagnostics graph.

item.title: Diagnostics Graph
item.description:
Unlike many validation libraries that return a flat list of error strings, FsFlow returns a structured Diagnostics Graph. This allows you to represent exactly where in a complex data structure a failure occurred.

The Structure

The graph is a tree where each node can contain local errors and child branches.

type PathSegment =
    | Key of string    // A record field or property
    | Index of int     // A position in a list or sequence
    | Name of string   // A custom label for a validation branch

type Diagnostic<'error> =
    { Path: PathSegment list
      Error: 'error }

type Diagnostics<'error> =
    { Errors: 'error list
      Children: Map<PathSegment, Diagnostics<'error>> }

Creating Scoped Diagnostics

Inside a validate {} block, you use helpers to push diagnostics into the tree:

item.title: FSharpPlus Integration
item.description:
This page shows how FsFlow can fit beside codebases that already use FSharpPlus.

FSharpPlus is a broad functional base library. That makes it different from the more targeted integration stories like FsToolkit.ErrorHandling and Validus.

FsFlow can sit on top of that style where orchestration begins.

FSharpPlus is strongest when the codebase already relies on broad generic operations and monad transformer stacks. That strength comes with a cost: more compiler work, more abstraction to keep in your head, and error surfaces that can be harder to read when you are trying to understand a FsFlow boundary.

item.title: Stream (FlowStream)
item.description:
FlowStream<'env, 'error, 'value> represents a cold, pull-based asynchronous stream. Like Flow, a FlowStream requires an environment to run and can fail with a typed error. It is built on top of .NET’s IAsyncEnumerable, providing native support for backpressure and asynchronous iteration.

Note: FlowStream is currently available on .NET only.

Creating a Stream

From a Sequence

The simplest way to create a stream is from an existing seq.

let numbers = FlowStream.fromSeq [ 1 .. 10 ]

Transforming Streams

Mapping Values

You can transform the successful values in a stream using FlowStream.map.

item.title: Tutorial: AppHost
item.description:
In enterprise .NET applications, you typically use a dependency injection (DI) container managed by IHostBuilder or IWebHostBuilder. FsFlow keeps that at the boundary: adapt the container into a plain record once, then use Flow with runtime overrides for clocking and logging. Those runtime services are implicit in normal flows and only become visible when you override them for tests or a local scope.

This tutorial shows a practical boundary:

  • IServiceProvider is only used in the host layer
  • your app dependencies stay in a typed record
  • runtime services like clock and log are implicit in normal flows, and Flow.withClock / Flow.withLog let you override them when you need test control

1. Register Services in the Container

Register your application services as you normally would.

item.title: Effect-TS Comparison
item.description:
This page shows FsFlow in relation to Effect-TS without pretending they solve the same problem at the same scale. The overlap is real, but FsFlow is intentionally much smaller and more F#-application-focused.

What Carries Over

These ideas are shared:

  • typed success and error channels
  • explicit dependency access
  • compositional workflow values
  • cancellation-aware execution
  • structured failure handling at the boundary

What Is Different

FsFlow is aimed at ordinary F# application code, especially the point where orchestration becomes visible:

item.title: Nominal Capability Contracts
item.description:
In FsFlow, the environment ('env) represents the “world” your effects need to run. Depending on the size of your application and your need for strictness, FsFlow provides three “levels” of environment access.

Level 1: Records (The Pragmatic Way)

For local logic or small applications, a plain F# record is often the best choice. It is direct, easy to mock, and requires zero ceremony.

Accessing via Flow.read

Use Flow.read to project a specific property from your environment record.

item.title: Standard .NET AppHost Plus DI
item.description:
IServiceProvider belongs at the outer edge of the system.

Using FsFlow with standard .NET Dependency Injection gives you:

  • Host registrations that you can turn into typed values once.
  • A familiar entry point for ASP.NET, Workers, and other DI-driven runtimes.
  • A bridge from dynamic host registrations to typed, honest core logic.

Pragmatic Access: Flow.inject

Flow.inject<'T>() pulls a service directly from the IServiceProvider in the environment.

let handleRequest = flow {
    let! db = Flow.inject<MyDbContext, _, _>()
    let! api = Flow.inject<IExternalApi, _, _>()
    // ...
}

This is the most ergonomic way to use FsFlow inside existing .NET applications.

item.title: Cause
item.description:
Represents the cause of a failed workflow.

Signature

type Cause<'error>

Type Parameters

Name
error

Union Cases

Case Description
Fail An expected domain-specific failure.
Die An unexpected defect or panic (e.g., an exception).
Interrupt An administrative signal to stop the workflow (e.g., cancellation).

item.title: Check
item.description:
Predicate helpers that return Result values with a unit error, plus the bridge functions that turn those checks into application errors. Some helpers preserve the source value; others are gates and return unit on success.

Signature

type Check

item.title: Effect
item.description:
Represents the portable execution shape used by the unified Flow.

Signature

type Effect<'value, 'error>

Type Parameters

Name
value
error

item.title: Exit
item.description:
Represents the final outcome of a workflow execution.

Signature

type Exit<'value, 'error>

Type Parameters

Name
value
error

Union Cases

Case Description
Success The workflow completed successfully.
Failure The workflow failed due to a specific cause.

item.title: Fiber
item.description:
Represents a handle to a workflow that has already been started.

Signature

type Fiber<'error, 'value>

Type Parameters

Name
error
value

Record Fields

Field Description
ExitTask The asynchronous operation that completes with the workflow’s final exit outcome.
InterruptSource The cancellation source used by Flow.interrupt to signal interruption.

Remarks

A fiber is the hot counterpart to a cold Flow. It keeps the running work's typed failure and success channels available through Flow.join, and it carries an interruption source so parent workflows can ask the child to stop and then wait for cleanup to finish.

item.title: Flow
item.description:
Represents a cold workflow that reads an environment, returns a typed result, and is executed explicitly through Flow.run.

Signature

type Flow<'env, 'error, 'value>

Type Parameters

Name
env
error
value

Union Cases

Case Description
Flow

item.title: FlowStream
item.description:
Represents a cold stream of values that requires an environment, can fail with a typed error, and supports backpressure.

Signature

Type Parameters

error

item.title: IHas
item.description:
Compatibility contract for a single dependency.

Signature

type IHas<'service>

Type Parameters

Name
service

Remarks

Prefer nominal capability interfaces for public workflows. This helper remains for lower-level binding machinery that still needs to work with a single dependency directly.

Examples

 type IDb =
     abstract Query : string -> string

 type AppEnv =
     { Database : IDb }
     interface IHas<IDb> with member x.Service = x.Database

 let db = Flow.service<IDb, AppEnv, unit>()
type IDb = abstract Query: string -> string
Multiple items
val string: value: 'T -> string

--------------------
type string = System.String
val x: AppEnv
AppEnv.Database: IDb
val db: obj
type AppEnv = { Database: IDb } interface obj override Service: IDb
type unit = Unit

item.title: PathSegment
item.description:
Location markers used to describe where a diagnostic belongs in a validation graph.

Signature

type PathSegment

Union Cases

Case Description
Key A string-based key, usually for map or record fields.
Index A zero-based integer index, usually for lists or arrays.
Name A descriptive name for a property or field.

Examples

 let s1 = PathSegment.Key "user-123"
 let s2 = PathSegment.Index 0
 let s3 = PathSegment.Name "Email"
val s1: obj
val s2: obj
val s3: obj

item.title: Ref
item.description:

T

Examples

     do! Ref.set 1 r
     let! v = Ref.get r
     return v
 }

item.title: Validation
item.description:
An accumulating validation result that keeps the structured diagnostics graph visible.

Signature

type Validation<'value, 'error>

Type Parameters

Name
value
error

Remarks

Unlike FSharpResult, this type is designed for applicative composition using and! in the validate { } builder, which merges errors instead of short-circuiting.

Examples

 let v1 = Validation.ok 5
 let v2 = Validation.error (Diagnostics.singleton "Error 1")
val v1: obj
val v2: obj

A single failure item attached to a path in a validation graph.

Signature

Type Parameters

Name
error

Record Fields

Field Description
Path The path to the source of the error.
Error The application-specific error value of type 'error.

Examples

val d: 'a
union case Result.Error: ErrorValue: 'TError -> Result<'T,'TError>

item.title: IGuid
item.description:
Provides synchronous GUID generation.

Signature

type IGuid

item.title: Diagnostics
item.description:
A mergeable validation graph that carries local errors and nested child branches.

Signature

type Diagnostics<'error>

Type Parameters

Name
error

Record Fields

Field Description
Errors Errors that occurred exactly at this node in the graph.
Children Nested diagnostic branches, keyed by PathSegment.

Remarks

Errors holds the application errors attached exactly to the current node, while Children holds nested branches keyed by PathSegment.

This structure allows hierarchical validation to stay navigable before flattening. Use flatten to convert it into a linear list.

Signature

type EnvironmentVariableError

Union Cases

Case Description

item.title: Flow.Runtime.cancellationToken
item.description:
Reads the current runtime cancellation token.

Signature

Flow.Runtime.cancellationToken

Returns

Type Description
Flow<‘env, ’error, CancellationToken> A flow that succeeds with the token supplied to runFull or runWithToken.

item.title: flow { }
item.description: This page shows the flow { } computation expression, the primary syntax for writing FsFlow workflows. Inside the builder, ordinary values, Result, Async, Task, Flow, and guarded sources can be sequenced without manually unwrapping each layer. The builder preserves the important boundaries: expected errors stay typed, defects become Cause.Die, cancellation becomes interruption, and environment access remains explicit through Flow.env or Flow.read. Prefer flow { } for application orchestration; keep pure validation and simple predicates in Check, Validation, or Result until the code needs environment or effects.

item.title: flow
item.description:
The universal flow { } computation expression.

Signature

flow

Returns

Type Description
FlowBuilder A FlowBuilder instance.

Remarks

Use this builder when the boundary can mix synchronous values, Async, Task, Result, and environment requests while keeping typed failures and explicit dependency access.

It preserves the current environment model while allowing the workflow to compose task-oriented inputs directly, so callers do not need to switch builders just to cross an async boundary.

item.title: result
item.description:
The fail-fast result { } computation expression.

Signature

result

Returns

Type Description
ResultBuilder A ResultBuilder instance.

Remarks

Use this builder when the happy path should short-circuit on the first error and you want to keep the workflow in Result shape all the way through.

It works well for parsing, validation, and other boundaries where failure is expected to stop the flow immediately instead of accumulating diagnostics.

Use Check.orError when a pure check needs a domain error, and Guard.MapError when you need to remap an existing error before entering the CE.

item.title: result
item.description:
The fail-fast result { } computation expression.

Signature

result

Returns

Type Description
ResultBuilder A ResultBuilder instance.

Remarks

Use this builder when the happy path should short-circuit on the first error and you want to keep the workflow in Result shape all the way through.

It works well for parsing, validation, and other boundaries where failure is expected to stop the flow immediately instead of accumulating diagnostics.

Use Check.orError when a pure check needs a domain error, and Guard.MapError when you need to remap an existing error before entering the CE.

item.title: validate { }
item.description: This page shows the validate { } computation expression for writing validation logic with direct, sequential syntax. The builder is best for validation steps that read clearly as a block while still returning Validation<'value, 'error>. Use it when each bound step depends on earlier successful values. For independent sibling fields where you want maximum error accumulation, prefer Validation.map2, map3, apply, collect, or traverseIndexed so all branches are evaluated and all diagnostics are retained.

item.title: validate
item.description:
The accumulating validate { } computation expression.

Signature

validate

Returns

Type Description
ValidateBuilder A ValidateBuilder instance.

Remarks

Use this builder when you want to collect all validation failures instead of stopping at the first one.

Use and! when sibling validations should accumulate into the same diagnostics graph. Plain let! and do! are sequential: if the left side fails, the later step is not evaluated.

Check<'value> covers both value-preserving checks and gate checks. Use Check.orError to attach an application error, and Guard.Of / Guard.MapError when you want the same error-bound source shape to participate directly in validation.

item.title: Flow.fork

Starts a flow in a new fiber without waiting for it to complete.

Returns

Remarks

Forking turns a cold flow description into hot child work and returns a handle that can later be joined or interrupted. Prefer zipPar or race when the caller only needs a simple parallel composition.

item.description:

Parameters

Returns

Type Description
Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> A Flow containing the projected value.

Remarks

This is the primary way to access app dependencies, configuration, or request metadata stored in env. The projection runs only when the flow is executed, so constructing the flow is still pure and side-effect free. Prefer small projections over passing a large environment deeper into reusable helpers.

item.title: Flow.join

Waits for a fiber to complete and returns its successful value or typed failure.

Flow.join fiber

Parameters

Name Type Description
fiber Fiber<‘error, ‘value> The fiber to join.

Returns

Type Description
Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> A flow that completes with the fiber's outcome.

Remarks

Joining preserves the child workflow's error channel. If the child failed with Cause.Fail, the joined flow fails with the same typed error; interruption and defects remain interruption and defects.

item.title: Diagnostics.merge
item.description:
Recursively merges two diagnostics graphs, combining shared branches and local errors.

Signature

Diagnostics.merge left right

Parameters

Name Type Description
left Diagnostics<’error> The first graph of type Diagnostics.
right Diagnostics<’error> The second graph of type Diagnostics.

Returns

Type Description
Diagnostics<’error> A new Diagnostics containing the union of both inputs.

Remarks

This is the core operation for applicative validation. It ensures that errors from sibling fields are collected together into a single structured graph.

Examples

 let d1 = Diagnostics.singleton "Error 1"
 let d2 = Diagnostics.singleton "Error 2"
 let combined = Diagnostics.merge d1 d2
val d1: obj
val d2: obj
val combined: obj

item.title: Diagnostics.toString
item.description:
Renders a diagnostics graph in a YAML-like layout for display.

Signature

Diagnostics.toString graph

Parameters

Name Type Description
graph Diagnostics<’error> The diagnostics graph to render.

Returns

Type Description
string A formatted string representation of the graph.

Remarks

This is intended for human-readable output. Empty sections are omitted, and children are shown directly under their branch labels at the same indentation level as errors. Errors render as YAML-style bullet items without an Errors: key. Use flatten when you need path-bearing diagnostics for reporting or assertions.

item.title: TRef.update
item.description:
Updates the value of the transactional reference within a transaction using the supplied function.

TRef.update f tref

tref TRef<‘T> The transactional reference to update.

Returns

Type Description
STM<unit> An STM operation that updates the reference value.

Examples

 let tx (counter: TRef<int>) = stm {
     do! TRef.update (fun n -> n + 1) counter
 }
val tx: counter: 'a -> 'b
val counter: 'a

item.title: Check.all
item.description:
Returns success when every check in the sequence succeeds.

Signature

Check.all checks

Parameters

Name Type Description
checks Check<‘value> seq A sequence of checks.

Returns

Type Description
Check<unit> A Check that succeeds only if all inputs succeed.

Remarks

Sequentially evaluates each check in the checks sequence. Stops at the first failure.

Examples

 [ Check.okIf true; Check.okIf true ] |> Check.all // Ok ()
 [ Check.okIf true; Check.okIf false ] |> Check.all // Error ()

item.title: STM.atomically
item.description:
Executes an STM transaction atomically within a flow while preserving retry/orElse coordination.

Signature

STM.atomically transaction

Parameters

Name Type Description
transaction STM<‘T> The STM transaction to execute.

Returns

Type Description
Flow<‘env, ’none, ‘T> A flow that performs the transaction and returns its result.

Remarks

FsFlow's STM uses a global synchronizing lock to ensure atomicity and coordinate retries. While this avoids complex optimistic concurrency control, it means that transactions are mutually exclusive and high contention can impact throughput.

item.title: Validation.fail
item.description:

item.title: Diagnostics.flatten
item.description:
Flattens the structured diagnostics graph into a linear list of diagnostics.

Signature

Diagnostics.flatten graph

Parameters

Name Type Description
graph Diagnostics<’error> The Diagnostics to flatten.

Returns

Type Description
Diagnostic<’error> list A list of type Diagnostic list.

Remarks

During flattening, child paths are accumulated from the root down into each emitted diagnostic. The tree itself stores only local errors and child branches, while Diagnostic is reserved for reporting output.

Examples

 let d = Diagnostics.singleton "fail"
 let flat = Diagnostics.flatten d
val d: obj
val flat: obj

from Microsoft.FSharp.Core

item.title: Validation.bind
item.description:
Sequences a validation-producing continuation.

Signature

Validation.bind binder validation

Parameters

validation Validation<‘value, ’error> The source validation.

Returns

Type Description

Remarks

This is the monadic "bind" for validation. Note that this operation short-circuits and does not accumulate errors from the binder if the source has already failed. For accumulation, use map2 or the applicative and! syntax.

item.title: Validation.map2
item.description:
Combines two validations, accumulating errors if both fail.

Signature

Validation.map2 mapper left right

Parameters

Name Type Description
mapper ‘left -> ‘right -> ‘value A function of type 'left -> 'right -> 'value.
left Validation<‘left, ’error> The first validation.
right Validation<‘right, ’error> The second validation.

Returns

Type Description
Validation<‘value, ’error> A validation with the combined result.

Remarks

This is the core applicative operation. If both left and right fail, their diagnostics graphs are merged.

Examples

 let v1 = Validation.ok 1
 let v2 = Validation.ok 2
 Validation.map2 (+) v1 v2 // Validation (Ok 3)
val v1: obj
val v2: obj

item.title: Validation.orElse
item.description:
Falls back to another validation when the source validation fails.

Validation.orElse fallback validation

Parameters

Name Type Description

validation Validation<‘value, ’error> The source validation.

Returns

Type Description

Remarks

This is a left-biased choice operator. If the source succeeds, the fallback is not used. If the source fails, the fallback validation is returned as-is.

item.title: Check.orError
item.description:
Maps a unit error into the supplied application error value.

Signature

Parameters

Name Type Description
error ‘error The domain error of type 'error to return on failure.

Returns

Type Description
Result<‘value, ’error> A Result with the provided error value.

Remarks

This is the primary bridge from checks to domain-specific results.

Examples

 "" |> Check.okIfNonEmptyStr |> Check.orError "Empty string" // Error "Empty string"
 "hello" |> Check.okIfNonEmptyStr |> Check.orError "Empty string" // Ok "hello"

item.title: Flow.run
item.description:
Executes a flow with the provided environment and the default cancellation token.

Signature

Flow.run environment flow

Parameters

Name Type Description
environment ‘env The environment required by the flow.
flow Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> The workflow to execute.

Returns

Type Description
Effect<‘value, ’error> An effect that represents the asynchronous execution outcome.

Remarks

Uncaught exceptions become Cause.Die; cancellation becomes Cause.Interrupt.

Examples

 let flow = Flow.read (fun env -> $"Hello, {env}!")
 let result = Flow.run "World" flow
 // result = Effect that resolves to Success "Hello, World!" on both .NET and Fable
val flow: obj
val result: obj

Lifts an option into a synchronous flow with the supplied error.

error ‘error The error to return if the option is None.

item.title: Flow.fromValueOption
item.description:
Lifts a value option into a synchronous flow with the supplied error.

Signature

Flow.fromValueOption error value

Parameters

item.title: Flow.orElseFlow
item.description:
Turns a pure validation result into a synchronous flow with environment-provided failure.

Signature

Flow.orElseFlow errorFlow result

Parameters

Name Type Description
errorFlow Flow<‘env, ’error, ’error> A flow that reads the environment to produce an error value.
result Result<‘value, unit> The pure result to bridge.

Returns

Type Description
Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> A Flow that mirrors the success of the result or fails with the outcome of the error flow.

Remarks

This helper bridges the gap between pure validation (which often uses Result or Check) and the Flow environment model. If the result is an error, the provided errorFlow is executed to produce the final application error.

item.title: Flow.env
item.description:
Reads the current environment as the successful flow value.

Signature

Flow.env

Returns

Type Description
Flow<‘env, ’error, ’env> A Flow whose successful value is the current environment.

Remarks

Use this when the next step genuinely needs the whole environment value, for example when passing a request context to another helper. For a single dependency or configuration value, prefer Flow.read; it keeps the dependency local and makes the workflow easier to scan.

Examples

 let myFlow = Flow.env |> Flow.map (fun env -> env)
val myFlow: obj

item.title: Flow.read
item.description:
Projects one value from the current environment.

Signature

Flow.read projection

Parameters

Name Type Description
projection ‘env -> ‘value A function that extracts a value from the environment.

Returns

Type Description
Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> A Flow containing the projected value.

Remarks

This is the primary way to access app dependencies, configuration, or request metadata stored in env. The projection runs only when the flow is executed, so constructing the flow is still pure and side-effect free. Prefer small projections over passing a large environment deeper into reusable helpers.

item.title: Flow.service
item.description:
Extracts a specific service from an environment that implements IHas<'service>.

Signature

Flow.service ()

Parameters

Name Type Description
() unit

Returns

Type Description
Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘service> A flow containing the requested service.

Remarks

This is the statically honest way to access dependencies.

Examples

 let flow = Flow.service<IMyService, _, _>()
val flow: obj

item.title: Flow.inject
item.description:
Injects a service from a dynamic IServiceProvider environment.

Signature

Flow.inject ()

Parameters

Name Type Description
() unit

Returns

Type Description
Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘service> A flow containing the requested service.

Remarks

Trades compile-time safety for pragmatic .NET interop.

Examples

 let flow = Flow.inject<IMyService, _, _>()
val flow: obj

item.title: Flow.map
item.description:
Transforms the successful value of a flow.

Signature

Flow.map mapper flow

Parameters

Name Type Description
mapper ‘value -> ’next A function of type 'value -> 'next to transform the successful value.
flow Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> The source flow of type Flow to transform.

Returns

Type Description
Flow<‘env, ’error, ’next> A new Flow with the transformed success value of type 'next.

Remarks

If the source flow fails, the mapper is not executed. The original failure cause is preserved, including typed failures, interruption, and defects. Use map for pure value transformations after an effect has succeeded.

item.title: Flow.bind
item.description:
Sequences a dependent flow after a successful value.

Signature

Flow.bind binder flow

Parameters

Name Type Description
binder ‘value -> Flow<‘env, ’error, ’next> A function that takes the successful value and returns a new flow.
flow Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> The source flow to sequence.

Returns

Type Description
Flow<‘env, ’error, ’next> A Flow representing the combined workflow.

Remarks

This is the flatmap operation for Flow. The continuation only runs when the source flow succeeds, and it receives the successful value. Use bind when the next effect depends on the previous result; use map when the next step is pure.

item.title: Flow.tap
item.description:
Runs an effect on success and preserves the original value.

Signature

Flow.tap binder flow

Parameters

Name Type Description
binder ‘value -> Flow<‘env, ’error, unit> A function that produces a side-effect flow from the successful value.
flow Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> The source flow.

Returns

Type Description
Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> A Flow that preserves the original success value after the side effect.

Remarks

Use this for logging, telemetry, metrics, or audit steps that should observe a successful value without replacing it. If the binder flow fails, that failure becomes the result of the whole flow, because the tap effect is still part of the workflow.

item.title: Flow.tapError
item.description:
Runs a synchronous side effect on failure and preserves the original error.

Signature

Flow.tapError binder flow

Parameters

Name Type Description
binder ‘error -> Flow<‘env, ’error, unit> A function that produces a side-effect flow from the error value.
flow Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> The source flow.

Returns

Type Description
Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> A Flow that preserves the original error after the side effect.

Remarks

Use this for error logging or cleanup actions that depend on the environment. If the binder side-effect flow itself fails, its error will overwrite the original error.

item.title: Flow.mapError
item.description:
Maps the error value of a synchronous flow.

Signature

Flow.mapError mapper flow

Parameters

Name Type Description
mapper ‘error -> ’nextError The function to transform the error value.
flow Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> The source flow.

Returns

Type Description
Flow<‘env, ’nextError, ‘value> A Flow with the transformed error type.

Remarks

Transforms the error type of the flow while leaving successful values untouched. Useful for mapping internal errors into public-facing domain errors.

Examples

 let flow = Flow.fail "error" |> Flow.mapError (fun err -> err + "!")
val flow: obj

item.title: Flow.catch
item.description:
Catches exceptions raised during execution and maps them to a typed error.

Signature

Flow.catch handler flow

Parameters

Name Type Description
handler exn -> ’error A function of type exn -> 'error to map the exception.
flow Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> The source flow of type Flow to monitor.

Returns

Type Description
Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> A Flow that converts exceptions into success-path errors.

Remarks

Exceptions that are not caught by this helper will bubble up to the caller of run. This ensures that known exceptions can be handled within the flow context.

item.title: Flow.orElseWith
item.description:
Computes a fallback flow from the typed error when the source flow fails.

Signature

Flow.orElseWith fallback flow

Parameters

Name Type Description
fallback ‘error -> Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> A function that produces a new flow from the error value.
flow Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> The source flow.

Returns

Type Description
Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> A flow that recovers from errors using the fallback function.

Remarks

The fallback runs only for expected typed failures represented by Cause.Fail. It does not catch interruption or defects. Use this for domain-level recovery, not for swallowing cancellation or unexpected exceptions.

item.title: Flow.orElse
item.description:
Falls back to another flow when the source flow fails.

Signature

Flow.orElse fallback flow

Parameters

Name Type Description
fallback Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> The flow to run if the source flow fails.
flow Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> The source flow.

Returns

Type Description
Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> A flow that recovers from errors using the fallback flow.

Examples

 let flow = Flow.fail "error" |> Flow.orElse (Flow.succeed "recovered")
val flow: obj

item.title: Flow.zip
item.description:
Runs two flows sequentially and combines their successful values into a tuple.

Signature

Flow.zip left right

Parameters

Name Type Description
left Flow<‘env, ’error, ’left> The first flow to run.
right Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘right> The second flow to run.

Returns

Type Description
Flow<‘env, ’error, (‘left * ‘right)> A flow that returns a tuple of both successful values.

Examples

 let flow = Flow.zip (Flow.succeed 1) (Flow.succeed 2)
val flow: obj

item.title: Flow.map2
item.description:
Combines two flows with a mapping function.

Signature

Flow.map2 mapper left right

Parameters

Name Type Description
mapper ‘left -> ‘right -> ‘value A function that combines the successful values of both flows.
left Flow<‘env, ’error, ’left> The first flow to run.
right Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘right> The second flow to run.

Returns

Type Description
Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> A flow containing the mapped value.

Examples

 let flow = Flow.map2 (fun x y -> x + y) (Flow.succeed 1) (Flow.succeed 2)
val flow: obj

item.title: Flow.map3
item.description:
Combines three flows with a mapping function.

Signature

Flow.map3 mapper left middle right

Parameters

Name Type Description
mapper ‘left -> ‘middle -> ‘right -> ‘value A function that combines the successful values of all three flows.
left Flow<‘env, ’error, ’left> The first flow to run.
middle Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘middle> The second flow to run.
right Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘right> The third flow to run.

Returns

Type Description
Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> A flow containing the mapped value.

Examples

 let flow = Flow.map3 (fun x y z -> x + y + z) (Flow.succeed 1) (Flow.succeed 2) (Flow.succeed 3)
val flow: obj

item.title: Flow.apply
item.description:
Applies a flow-wrapped function to a flow-wrapped value.

Signature

Flow.apply flow value

Parameters

Name Type Description
flow Flow<‘env, ’error, (‘value -> ’next)> A flow that contains a function to apply.
value Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> A flow that contains the value to apply the function to.

Returns

Type Description
Flow<‘env, ’error, ’next> A flow containing the result of applying the function to the value.

Examples

 let flow = Flow.apply (Flow.succeed (fun x -> x + 1)) (Flow.succeed 1)
val flow: obj

item.title: Flow.ignore
item.description:
Maps the successful value of a synchronous flow to unit.

Signature

Flow.ignore flow

Parameters

Name Type Description
flow Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> The source flow.

Returns

Type Description
Flow<‘env, ’error, unit> A flow that succeeds with unit instead of the original value.

Examples

 let flow = Flow.succeed 42 |> Flow.ignore
val flow: obj

item.title: Flow.localEnv
item.description:
Runs a flow against an environment derived from the outer environment.

Signature

Flow.localEnv mapping flow

Parameters

Name Type Description
mapping ‘outerEnvironment -> ‘innerEnvironment A function that maps the outer environment to the inner environment.
flow Flow<‘innerEnvironment, ’error, ‘value> The flow to run with the inner environment.

Returns

Type Description
Flow<‘outerEnvironment, ’error, ‘value> A flow that expects the outer environment.

Remarks

Use this to embed a smaller workflow inside a larger application environment without changing the smaller workflow's type. The mapping is applied at execution time. This is useful for preserving narrow helper signatures while still running everything from one app boundary.

item.title: Flow.provideLayer
item.description:
Runs a layer flow first, then runs a downstream flow with the layer's output as its environment.

Signature

Flow.provideLayer layer flow

Parameters

Name Type Description
layer Flow<‘input, ’error, ’environment> A flow that provides the environment required by the downstream flow.
flow Flow<‘environment, ’error, ‘value> The flow to run with the provided environment.

Returns

Type Description
Flow<‘input, ’error, ‘value> A flow that requires only the input environment of the layer.

Remarks

Use this at composition boundaries where one flow builds the environment needed by another flow. Ordinary workflow code should usually consume an environment directly with Flow.read; provideLayer is for deriving or provisioning an environment before a downstream workflow starts.

item.title: Flow.delay
item.description:
Defers flow construction until execution time.

Signature

Flow.delay factory

Parameters

Name Type Description
factory unit -> Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> A function that returns the flow to execute.

Returns

Type Description
Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> A flow that lazily evaluates the factory when executed.

Examples

 let flow = Flow.delay (fun () -> Flow.succeed 42)
val flow: obj

item.title: Flow.traverse
item.description:
Transforms a sequence of values into a flow and stops at the first failure.

Signature

Flow.traverse mapping values

Parameters

Name Type Description
mapping ‘value -> Flow<‘env, ’error, ’next> A function that maps each value to a flow.
values ‘value seq The sequence of values to transform.

Returns

Type Description
Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘next list> A flow containing a list of the successful mapped values.

Examples

 let flows = [1; 2; 3] |> Flow.traverse (fun x -> Flow.succeed (x * 2))
val flows: obj

item.title: Flow.sequence
item.description:
Transforms a sequence of flows into a flow of a sequence and stops at the first failure.

Signature

Flow.sequence flows

Parameters

Name Type Description
flows Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> seq The sequence of flows to run.

Returns

Type Description
Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value list> A flow containing a list of the successful values.

Examples

 let flow = Flow.sequence [Flow.succeed 1; Flow.succeed 2]
val flow: obj

item.title: Capabilities.Core.EnvironmentVariables.tryGet
item.description:
Reads a raw environment-variable value from the ambient runtime.

Signature

Capabilities.Core.EnvironmentVariables.tryGet name

Parameters

Name Type Description
name string The name of the environment variable.

Returns

Type Description
Flow<‘env, ’e, string option> A flow that produces the variable value if it exists.

item.title: Flow.zipPar
item.description:
Combines two flows into a tuple of their values, running them concurrently.

Signature

Flow.zipPar left right

Parameters

Name Type Description
left Flow<‘env, ’error, ’left> The first flow to combine.
right Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘right> The second flow to combine.

Returns

Type Description
Flow<‘env, ’error, (‘left * ‘right)> A flow that returns a tuple of both successful values.

Remarks

If either flow fails, the other is interrupted immediately.

Examples

 let combined = Flow.zipPar flow1 flow2
val combined: obj

item.title: Capabilities.Core.EnvironmentVariables.live
item.description:
Creates a live provider backed by the current process environment.

Signature

Capabilities.Core.EnvironmentVariables.live

Returns

Type Description
IEnvironmentVariables A live IEnvironmentVariables implementation.

item.title: Flow.race
item.description:
Runs two flows concurrently and returns the result of the first one to complete.

Signature

Flow.race left right

Parameters

Name Type Description
left Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> The first flow to run.
right Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> The second flow to run.

Returns

Type Description
Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> A flow containing the result of the first flow to complete.

Remarks

The "loser" flow is interrupted immediately.

Examples

 let fastOrSlow = Flow.race fastFlow slowFlow
val fastOrSlow: obj

Capabilities.Core.EnvironmentVariables.fromPairs values

Type Description
IEnvironmentVariables A mock IEnvironmentVariables implementation.

item.title: Capabilities.Core.EnvironmentVariableErrors.describe
item.description:
Formats a human-readable description for an error.

Signature

Capabilities.Core.EnvironmentVariableErrors.describe _arg1

Parameters

Name Type Description
_arg1 EnvironmentVariableError

Returns

Type Description
string A human-readable error message.

item.title: Flow.Retry
item.description:
Retries a failing flow according to the supplied schedule.

Signature

Flow.Retry(flow, schedule)

Parameters

Name Type Description
flow Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> The workflow to retry if it fails.
schedule Schedule<‘env, ’error, ‘output> The schedule that determines when and if to retry based on the error.

Returns

Type Description
Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> A flow that will retry the original flow according to the schedule until it succeeds or the schedule stops.

Examples

 let flakyWork = Flow.fail "oops"
 let retried = Flow.Retry(flakyWork, Schedule.recurs 3)
val flakyWork: obj
val retried: obj

item.title: Flow.Repeat
item.description:
Repeats a successful flow according to the supplied schedule.

Signature

Flow.Repeat(flow, schedule)

Parameters

Name Type Description
flow Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> The workflow to repeat if it succeeds.
schedule Schedule<‘env, ‘value, ‘output> The schedule that determines when and if to repeat based on the successful value.

Returns

Type Description
Flow<‘env, ’error, ‘value> A flow that repeats the original flow according to the schedule, returning the last successful value when it stops.

Examples

 let work = Flow.ok 42
 let repeated = Flow.Repeat(work, Schedule.recurs 5)
val work: obj
val repeated: obj
